title: 2026/02/25 GA Subcommittee A [Part 1]
displayTitle: 2026-02-25 - Governance and Administration Committee - Emergency Management Bill [Subcommittee A, Part 1]
description: 25 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A Emergency Management Bill (No 2) Save the Children New Zealand - Jacqui Southey, Child Rights Advocacy and Research Director Lianne Dalziel Te Kahui Tika Tangata NZ Human Rights Commission - Prudence Walker, Disability Rights Commissioner Environment Southland - Rob Phillips, Acting Chief Executive - Phil Morrison, Deputy-Chair Hui E! Community Aotearoa - Patrick Gemmell, Kaiwhakahaere Matua | Chief Executive, Tangata Whenua - Dr. Katie Bruce, Kaiwhakahaere Matua | Chief Executive, Tangata Tiriti Dunedin City Council - Mike Costelloe, General Manager, Arts, Culture & Economic Development - Danielle Tolson, Corporate Policy Analyst Andrea Beard Inner City Wellington Residents Association - Rev Stephen King, Chair Karyn Ward
descriptionSummary: Emergency Management Bill. Emergency Management Bill (No 2). Save the Children New Zealand

chunks.text: There, Jackie, is that correct? Yes. Kilda. Kilda. Welcome. Thank you so much for joining us. You're our first sub submitter this morning, and what's a, what's a very full day of submission, so we're excited to hear from you. We do have a copy of your written, uh, submission that we have the opportunity to look at. Uh, and we've got 10 minutes today to hear from you. So welcome. You're welcome to use all of that time or save some time for questions from members at the end, if there are any over to you. Sure.
Thank you.

Child Rights Advocacy and Research Director for Save the Children. Thank you for the opportunity to share our oral submission on the emergency management system. Bill number two, and Save the Children has more than 100 years of experience in responding to emergencies with a specific focus on the rights needs and wellbeing of children.

We were founded in 1919 in response to the P of Children in Europe, terribly affected by the aftermath of World War I, and this was our first emergency response. We have an extensive history of more than 50 years working with the New Zealand government and ODA and Emergency response, and primarily in overseas settings.

And some recent examples include Fiji, Vanuatu, Solomon Islands, and Tonga, and Principles Save the Children's supports the Emergency Management Bill. We are strongly supportive of the Bill's intention to modernize and redevelop New Zealand's emergency management system to ensure that it is fit for purpose and can meet the reality that Tua New Zealand is facing more frequent and more severe weather events.

And our submission we make for key recommendations. And the first of these is to explicitly include and reference children as a key stakeholder group in the bill through appointing a person with knowledge, experience, and expertise related to children and the emergency management coordinating executive groups.

Currently, the bill does not adequately identify or include children as a key stakeholder group, despite children being at greater risk during and after an emergency event. Sadly, nine people lost their lives in emergency events in the east of the North Island in just January, in January this year alone. And of those nine three were children.

It is critical children have greater visibility and recognition in the four key stages of emergency management readiness, risk reduction, response and recovery. In clause 39 of the bill, it requires the establishment of emergency management coordinating executive, uh, groups, and it clearly states the requirements of the membership group to hold expertise related to health disability, local Maori and Iwi representation and the needs of rural communities.

Among other requirements. Expertise related to children is not a stated requirement. It is the view of Save the Children. Based on our extensive experience in the field responding to emergencies, that expert representation of children should be stated in the bill as a requirement of these groups. A second recommendation is to take a child rights approach to emergency management al New Zealand and amend the current bill to specifically include reference to upholding the rights of children throughout the manage, sorry, emergency management system.

The increasing frequency and severity of weather related events is putting children at risk globally. Children are particularly vulnerable to extreme climate related events, economic, social and environmental shocks and disasters, their rights to a healthy and safe environment. Life and survival development, health, food, water, and sanitation.

Social protection and adequate standard of living education and non-discrimination are increasingly hindered by the detri detrimental effects of climate change, including water scarcity, undernourishment, and significant increase in frequency and intensity of weather related emergencies. Taking a child rights approach, ensure children are considered and accommodated as key as a key stakeholder group, and the conventional rights of the child serves as a useful framework to ensure that children's needs and wellbeing are met during

and in the recovery phase of an emergency event. Key components of a child rights approach include protection, participation, provision, reunification, and psychosocial support. The bill should be our third recommendation is the bill should be strengthened to give greater attention to readiness in the emergency management system.

Currently, the bill states requirements for risk reduction through risk identification and management response through clarifying roles, responsibilities and legal requirements and recovery through transition periods and legal exercise of powers and allocation of resources. However, readiness is largely addressed through planning requirements yet to be put in place.

Save the children supports recommendations by the inquiry into the response of the North Island severe weather offense in 2023 to strengthen readiness requirements, particularly at the local level based on reported experience of people in affected communities. The inquiry found that there is an opportunity to strengthen the critical role communities play in emergency response, including through ongoing public education programs.

There is also a need to significantly improve with in CDM agencies, especially in readiness activities and community engagement. There is a disconnect between ceds expectations and understandings and that of community expectations and understandings to achieve this, the resourcing of emergency preparedness and education is critically needed at the community level.

Our final recommendation is to ensure adequate resourcing led by central government and including local government to give effect to the new fit for purpose emergency management system Resourcing, including exp expertise, capacity and financial investment must be allocated of a new emergency management system, is to be effective in addressing the growing frequency and severity of emergency events in al investment and education at the community and household level is to ensure that we are better prepared as a nation the need for education as part

of emergency preparedness as well. Recognize Save the Children conducts emergency preparedness education programs around the world, including through programs that delivered in New Zealand, the Pacific and Asia Regions monitoring and evaluation of emergency preparedness education programs show that schools and communities that engage in preparedness education are better prepared to cope, to cope with, and also recover from emergency events.
That concludes our submission, and I'm happy to take any questions.

I think you're on mute. I think you're pointing to me chair. Um, but you're on mute, so I'll just ask my question. Um, Sorry. Um, I just, um, have trouble with my mouse. Um, but yes, Fran, please ask. Okay. Thank, thank you chair. Um, thank you so much for your submission, Jackie. I'm, I'm, I'm interested in the point you made around, um, 4.1, uh, around, um, making sure that there's, um, someone with, uh, expertise like with children in these groups.

Are you talking about direct lived experience through like youth council or a kind of more facilitated model, like through the children's commissioner, for example? Well, I think the expertise will be because these are community groups will be sourced at the community level. And so it could be, uh, people who are professionals working with or for children.

So it could be somebody, uh, in the teaching sector who who is aware of, uh, 'cause often schools and have a quite a strong program of emergency preparedness within the school. Uh, it could be, um, uh, experts within the community that put their hand up or can be identified. I don't think we need to say it's exactly this one person.

It could be a youth group if there is a strong youth council within the community network that could certainly, um, provide advice and support. And I think it's important to recognize that children are not homogenous and that there are ages and stages of development within children. So if we're planning for emergencies, how are we planning for infants to be cared for?

Infants are, are a different, um, set of circumstances to say adolescents, uh, partic, particularly girls going through pub puberty that might need support in that way. And then of course, there are children that, um, may have specific health needs, uh, like adults do, but really, you know, children are completely reliant on adults with their support, care and care in particularly in the younger years.

So having expertise stated just as it's stated for those other groups like Iwi, Maori, uh, and also I think there's rural and the others that I mentioned it, it's not prescriptive to say who it will be, but it is stated as a requirement, as part of these groups as an, as holding that expertise. Thank you. Thanks.

I just also had a question around, um, kind of two questions. The first one you might be able to answer quickly, which is just the list that you've got in your written submission around the, uh, child rights approach to emergency management. Do you think that that could be incorporated, um, or were you imagining that that would be in the bill or that that would be kind of in codes of practice or, or in how, um, individual civil defense groups dealt with particular emergencies?

Like where would you see that being kind of codified? I think there's a place for it in both. And even if it's, um, a smaller statement in the bill that then, um, as it, it becomes a requirement if it's legislated, it's a legal requirement rather than a nice to have or a should have, it would have great, you know, very positive effect in those, um, codes of conduct and in the emergency management plans that are yet to be developed.

However, I think there's a place for both because there's a, a possibility of missing, uh, that requirement if there's not a reference to upholding the rights of children or, or that reference to take a child rights emergency management approach somewhere. Um, um, there's a number of places that it could sit in the bill. Fantastic. We are actually out of time now, so I'll end it there. But thank you so much for joining us, uh, this morning and for your written submission as well.

We really appreciate your time. Thank you. Thank you very much. Appreciate the opportunity. Kaki Kaki. Our next submitter is Leanne Darle, and I think you're there, Leanne. I am indeed. Fantastic. We've, We've only got got five minutes with you and I know you have a lot of expertise. We've got a very good written submission from you.

So, um, I won't, I won't delay, but I'll hand over to you. Thank you. And I, I, yeah, we'll mention that in a minute. Uh, Sunday was the 15th anniversary of the 22nd of February earthquake, 185 people died. A hundred and 15th of them were in one building. And I kind of wanted the committee to think about whether the legislation that they're considering today would've made any sort of difference.

And I'm gonna say, sadly, don't hold your breath. This bill doesn't cover what happened. Uh, and, and on that occasion, it was the building act, um, and the issuing of placards red, yellow, and, uh, white. It is today, but on the 4th of September, 2010, shortly after that, when the CTV building was stickered, it was a green sticker that it received.

And the point that I'm making here is that there are, um, a lot of silos sitting in this space, and I just want to encourage you to look at those silos and to think about how they are all connected. Unless there is a better appreciation of that in a time of crisis, we won't learn the lessons that we need to, and we will repeat the same mistakes because I hate to say it, we have been repeating the same mistakes for a long time, and particularly in the area of recovery as well.

Of the others who died on February the 22nd, more than half of them were killed by falling unreinforced masonry. It wasn't tied back or it wasn't cordon properly, excuse me, after the 4th of September earthquake, the first people who died in the Hawke's Bay earthquake, February the third, 1931, almost 80 years to the day were killed by falling masonry.

Why don't we learn the lessons of our experiences? And I wanted to grab your attention with that statement, because I only have five minutes, and as you'll see from my submission, I support the changes that are being made in the bill. But I but don't think for a minute that this is world leading.

And it could be, we really could step up here if we don't have a process in place to learn the lessons from our previous experiences. If there is not a requirement for a fully independent inquiry that does not have the terms of written terms of reference written by those who are being, um, scrutinized, if there is no obligation to embed any lessons learned into future practice, if there is no centralized repository of the rec recommendations, we will continue.

Uh, to repeat the same states time and time again, I've rec recommended the independent general emergency, I'm sorry, inspector General of emergency management, uh, management approach, um, that has been adopted in a couple of the states and in Australia. And I know that, uh, one of the first, I think, um, uh, independent inspector generals rather, um, he has, I believe, made a submission to this committee as well.

Um, or, and as I've actually written to the Prime Minister about this, because it was an idea that was brought to my attention when I wrote a column for, for, um, about it on newsroom, um, that a standing commission of inquiry into adverse offense, uh, be set up. And I guess I'm gonna run out of time, so I'm just gonna say there are four s in this act.

Um, it covers two of them, readiness and response. It does not cover risk reduction in a meaningful way, and it doesn't recover recovery. Major disasters will, um, requiring an emergency response will always be followed by a recovery. So why aren't we better prepared? And in terms of risk reduction, it's been 15 years since the Canterbury earthquake sequence.

What, so why are we still discussing taking people out of harm's way? Um, sometimes referred to, um, as managed retreat in terms of recovery, we need people prepared to go in and to support straight away so that the emergency operation center can stay focused on response.

But recovery, the recovery function operates in parallel. So it's got to get underway straight away. And, uh, that's where you need external expertise to come in and support, but with sufficient knowledge and experience to be able to work, um, very collaboratively, um, across the particular region that they might begin.

So I'm afraid of running out of time, so I'll stop there. Thank you so much. And I'd have a good look at your submission, uh, yesterday. And, um, just a one quick question, um, if you have time, is the, uh, ideas around having a standing review is, I mean, that's, I take your point around the, the nature of the act and the fact it's, um, not going as far as, um, it would need to go to address some of the concerns that you've raised, but that having a standing review or having a more automatic review function is something

that we could potentially look at as an amendment to this, um, bill as it's drafted at the moment. Is that your view? I think that, I think you should, um, I think you should look at it, and even if it were not possible to be addressed, uh, um, in this bill, um, a recommendation that, uh, some detailed analysis of the, um, approach that's been adopted in Australia, you know, I mean, I, I'd rather that things were done well rather than quickly.

It has been 15 years. We haven't, uh, learned the lessons of our experience in in Christchurch. Um, I would be happy to provide you with some supplementary information on that particular issue, if that would, it would, if it would assist That, that would be excellent. And, uh, yeah, sorry for the short amount of time, um, that we have with you today.

Uh, but that would, that would be really helpful. And thanks also, Leanne, for, um, uh, mentioning the Ann 15 year anniversary and the 185 people that were lost in Christchurch. I think that's, um, uh, something that's really important for the committee to have in mind when we are considering this bill. So thank you so much for your submission and for your time today. Thank you very much. Thank You. All right. We now have, uh, prudence Walker from the Human Rights Commission.
Levi is with us,
or, or Francis

Kilda Kilda Prudent. Welcome. Thank you for joining us. Thank you. We've got a copy of your submission in 10 minutes to hear from you, so I'll leave it over to you and, and your team for your submission. Awesome. Thank you. Thank you for this opportunity to expand on our, uh, written submission. If we want an emergency management system that can be relied on and that we know will take care of everyone in our communities.

This bill needs to mandate for iwi involvement, disabled people's involvement and accessibility standards. We support the progress the bill makes towards this through improved governance of the emergency management system through recognition of the vital role played by Iwi Hapu Marai, and through recognition of disproportionate impact on some communities in times of crisis.

However, this recognition needs to be matched with formalized authority for decision making, giving effect to the rights of participation and appropriate resourcing for the system to facilitate us all working together for the common good. We cannot rely on discretionary involvement of iwi, Maori, or disproportionately affected communities.

Discretionary involvement is neither effective nor meets Titi or Waitangi or human rights standards. This bill sets the foundation for any legislation or regulations, so must embed Titi and Human Rights. Unambiguously active involvement of iwi Maori as to Titi partners needs to be clearly stated and the purpose of the bill, and in all clauses related to the composition of governance structures and planning and decision making bodies such as emergency management committees.

Alongside these provisions, requisite resourcing for participation and the essential infrastructure provided needs to be included in the bill. The vital role that Iwi and Hapu play across the four Rs is reflected in the many reports informing this legislation and has again, been acknowledged by responsible ministers in statements about recent weather events.

So it is a significant emission that the bill fails to explicitly recognize or to embed, uh, or to embed practical application of self-determination or partnerships. We support the submission of the Iwis Forum and commend that to you as a guide for how relevant clauses could be articulated throughout the bill, within the broad mandate of Te Kahui Tangata to promote, protect, and advocate respect for human rights and the human rights dimensions of Titi.

My role as Disability Rights Commissioner gives specific attention to the rights of disabled people. Disabled people are demonstrably one of the communities disproportionately affected in emergencies. That disproportionate impact arises in large part from being invisible, disregarded, or prevented from participating in preparation.

So to improve the design and responsiveness of the system, disabled people must be made visible and explicitly named in the legislation with expanded guidance about who disproportionately effective communities refers to, along with other groups that we've specified in our submission.

From my recent discussions with local government, this is guidance. I think responsible bodies would welcome to ensure that disproportionate impact is mitigated. The bill must require emergency management committees to actively include disabled people and their representative organizations alongside other disproportionately affected communities.

Lack of engagement by emergency management bodies is a consistent theme I hear in many of my conversations with disabled people and Tang Maori. And for the latter, this can be both as Maori and as disabled people, I acknowledge that there are pockets of good practice for engagement with disabled people and access to information, but this is a only in pockets, and b, focused largely on personal preparedness.

This bill needs to strengthen system preparedness. The bill needs to provide a system lever requiring emergency management committees and first responders to consistently plan with and for disabled people.

There needs to be a very well oiled machine to operate effectively in the disruptions of an emergency. It takes time to build that, to build strategic and trusting relationships with disabled people to understand and plan for inclusive and accessible processes, information and communication channels.

We have seen increased capacity, the increased system capacity for accessible information and alternate formats, but without a clear mandate experience shows that priority or resource will not be directed to maintaining initiatives and relationships that arise immediately after emergencies.

Participation is not a privilege, it is a right and the processes that will facilitate that need to be embedded and resourced at a systems level, we welcome the recognition of barriers and for some communi, for some communities, and recognize that the emergency management system may not be able to solve all of the barriers, disabled peace people in Tang, Maori face, such as material hardship.

But to effectively mitigate their impact and emergencies and to uphold human rights standards, all legislative policy and investment levers must be deployed to reduce barriers. I do want to leave time for any questions you have. So I will conclude by reiterating that the best way to mitigate risk and ensure we have a robust and resilient system is to embed Titi.

The most effective way to achieve this is to explicitly include the rights of governance and participation of iwi in the bills provisions for planning and designing emergency management systems with UA and communities. We will all benefit if the diverse experiences, knowledge, generosity, and intergenerational thinking of UA and community, become the recognized foundation of our emergency management system.

And that must include disabled people. Thank you so much. We do have a a little bit of time for questions. I just had a question around, um, the best way for local communities to be aware of the needs of this disabled community. So, um, what would your advice be around that?

Would, would it be, um, that, how, how would it, I think most of these emergencies that we have, uh, addressed on quite a local level. And so, uh, how would that engagement happen so that disabled members of the public feel confident that people know what their needs are and also that there's a, a appropriate dialogue.

Um, so if an emergency did happen, they'd be able to receive the appropriate assistance. I think it's at every level, um, that disabled people need to be involved. So recognizing, uh, the role of local authorities and local, um, communities, um, but also, uh, at every level of the system, we need to create space for that.

Um, so in terms of engagement at local levels, um, there has to be connection in local communities. Um, but the infrastructure and support at a national level would be a good start. Um, and, uh, also to look to disabled persons organizations, um, for the first points of contact around that.

Fantastic. Thank you so much. Thanks, uh, for both joining us today and for your, for your submission. We'll definitely make sure that we raise, um, those, uh, issues that you have, uh, raised with us with officials afterwards. Thank you so much, Kiara. Kiara, right. Our next submitter is Environment. Southland,

welcome to unmute yourself. Welcome. Thank you so much for joining us today on the Emergency Management Bill. Uh, we've received your written submission. We've got 10 minutes to hear from you, and if you want to leave time for questions at the end, um, you may, uh, hand over to you, Kira. Yeah, No, thank you. And I'm hoping you're hearing me clearly.

Um, Kira Coto, I'm Phil Morrison, deputy Chair of Environment South, and I'm joined by our chief executive Rob Phillips and, uh, general Manager integrated catchment management, Lucy Hicks, who has significant experience as an emergency management controller. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today on behalf of Environment Southland, about the emergency management Bill.

And I wish to acknowledge and thank you for your collaborative mahi on what is a strategically important bill for regions such as, uh, Southland Emergency Management in Southland is hosted by Environment Southland as a shared regional model with four councils jointly responsible and supported by MANA Emergency Services and community stakeholders on an increasingly regular basis.

Our region deals with slow developing flood related and climate driven emergencies. In this context, the bill is an important step forward, and we support its general intent. However, there are several changes we believe are needed to ensure the legislation works well in practice, delivering on that intent, uh, relating to essential infrastructure, we are asking that the bill clearly recognizes flood protection networks, including flood banks, as essential infrastructure for Southland Flood Banks, uh, fundamental assets

with a critical role in emergencies. They protect life, property access, economic activity, and essential lifelines across rural communities, towns, and our city. As drafted. The definition of essential infrastructure in Clause seven does not explicitly include them. We recommending, uh, amending clause seven or the associated schedule so that flood protection networks are expressly recognized as essential infrastructure.

This would align the bill with the national policy statement for infrastructure and give councils clearer authority and consistency when maintaining or appearing these assets during emergencies and recovery. For example, during the September, 2023, flooding gore in Gore, rising Rivers put pressure on key transport routes led to evacuations and exposed gaps in communication and declaration processes, highlighting how essential well maintained flood protection infrastructure is for keeping our communities connected and for reducing wider disruption.

With regard to group, office and group manager roles as drafted, clause 38 2 currently describes group offices as providing administrative and related services, and we feel that description does not reflect the reality of modern emergency management.

Our group office provides hazard intelligence planning readiness activities, coordinated multi-agency work, community engagement, and iwi partnership support. And these are specialist professional functions that enable the emergency management system to operate effectively. Therefore, we recommend amending Clause 38 2, so that the group officers are recognized for their professional and technical functions, and that national guidance be developed to clarify their role responsibilities and minimum capability expectations.

This will ensure national consistency and support the increasing demands created by climate driven hazards for iwi and Maori participation. We believe the bill needs stronger wording around iwi partnership. Clause 39 2 F refers to appointing individuals who reflect local perspectives of Maori, and we feel that phrase is too broad and risks enabling representation that actually does not reflect UA authority.

Recent emergencies have shown how vital Iwi involvement is for welfare support, local response and informed decision making. A clear example of this is the Oua tuna wetlands fire in 2022, where AKA Oua worked closely with emergency services and they provided place-based cultural and environmental knowledge to support the response demonstrating the importance of UA involvement.

So we recommend amending Clause 39 2 F so that representation is grounded in UA authority. We also recommend that the bill recognize the critical role Marai play during emergencies and include clearer expectations for the protection of Tonga Maori.

For early planning that supports culturally appropriate responses, uh, we are keen to see more recognition for developing emergencies in Southland. Many emergencies begin slowly whether patterns build rivers rise, access roots become vulnerable, and communities and agencies begin to take action before a declaration occurs.

While the bill provides clarity for declared emergency, it provides limited guidance for this important early stage councils can face uncertain thresholds for activation, unclear liability risks, and a lack of clarity. For community volunteers and partner agencies who often need to act early to reduce emerging risks, we recommend clarifying expectations for early pre declaration coordination, providing liability protection for councils and volunteers, acting in good faith and offering guidance on how early stage actions should be resourced or funded.

Clearer direction will support more timely and consistent national practice, especially for slow onset events. And this will help reduce overall response and recovery recovery costs by enabling better coordinated action. So we're seeking to be proactive without being alarmist.

Implementation and resourcing will be critical. The bill creates significant new responsibilities, including strengthened iwi partnership, expanded planning and readiness duties, and increased expectations for coordination and community engagement. Emergency management is currently funded almost entirely by local government, and these new demands would place an untenable burden on councils, especially a future constraints on council revenue are introduced.

We recommend including a provision that ensures central government funding for the additional responsibilities created by the bill, supported by clear national guidance and staged implementation. Without sustainable funding, the objectives of the bill are unlikely to be achieved.

In closing, we to summarize, we support the intent of the bill and ask the committee to consider these five refinements. One, explicitly recognizing flood protection networks as in essential infrastructure. Two, strengthening the role and capability expectations of group officers and group managers. Three, grounding iwi representation in UA authority, and recognizing the role of marai and protection of Tom La Maori.

Four, providing clearer guidance for managing, developing emergencies prior to a declared emergency. And five, establishing a sustainable national funding pathway for the expanded responsibilities in the bill. These refinements would ensure legislation reflects the operational realities we face and strengthen the emergency management system for the communities we serve.

Nai uh, thank you for the opportunity to present today and the team are happy to respond to any questions you may have. Thank you. Uh, thanks so much for that, um, submission and, um, it was great to be able to see, um, how effective the operation was, um, uh, during the last emergency that was, that was down there, that seemed to be handled really well.

I know that the risk suggests that it's something like $83.5 million of costs nationwide, but, um, of that, how, like, how much would environment is that environment?

Southland, thank you. Yeah, thank you. Um, in response to the first question, we, we do not know yet if there is, uh, if there is provision there. And in the second one, I believe that we have not yet, uh, committed to calculating the specific costs of that we would incur, um, as depending on the shape of the new bill of the new act.

Thank you so much. Uh, we have, uh, now, uh, used all, all of our time, so thank you so much for joining us and we really appreciate the, especially the detailed recommendations you have on the bill that's, um, super helpful as well. So appreciate that work. Thank you. Pleasure. Thank you. Thank you so much. Uh, we now have HU community and I understand we've got Katie and Patrick joining us.
Welcome.

Over to you, Katie. Um, it's a real pleasure to be, um, with you today and to be submitting, um, our all, um, opportunity, um, with you this morning on this bill. Thank you for that. 17 member organizations of the community Constellation that who year convene have made this submission to you today.

They are peak bodies from across the sector and capability building organizations all saying the same thing, um, to you this morning. The recent flooding and landslide events, as we all know, have demonstrated the critical role that the Tua, um, community, um, and voluntary sector play in emergency response and recovery often before government arrive and certainly long after they leave.

These events have reinforced the urgency of ensuring that community voices knowledge and resources are formally recognized and adequately resourced within New Zealand's emergency management system. So our submission makes a number of recommendations that are about investing in community infrastructure to enable this to be a framework for resilience, response and recovery, not just rescue.

These include embedding community and volunteer expertise in emergency management governance, con continuity planning for essential community services and developing community resilience metrics. So this bill establishes a framework for emergency management functions and accountability, but it doesn't establish funding streams for UA and community organizations to prepare for emergencies or to respond when they occur.

Community resilience comes from being community led, community rooted, not just from external agency coordination alone. Over to you Patrick. Um, just with regards to, uh, the ability for Maori to, uh, respond to crisis and emergency, um, unfortunately Maori know this all too well, um, is deeply rooted in having to plan for leadership loss.

And we've seen that with the loss of the Maori king and also Ari Kiwi, where, uh, a whole bunch of people had to drop tools and respond to a, to a crisis. So, uh, grief, confusion, community disintegration, and then they have to go and seek, uh, resources to host the hundreds of people without literally no notice, uh, to continue to provide, uh, community cohesion, uh, and also having to adhere to adhere to their cultural convention of man.

So providing assistance to perform, perform their duty of care, their cultural duty of care to those who re require it nonetheless. So, uh, I've seen it all too. Well, I'm based in the north, and so those marai, uh, on the LA coastline Nazi had to respond to, uh, the needs of the community and adhere to their cultural convention.

So ideally, uh, our key recommendations outline, uh, what we're looking to, uh, to advocate for, uh, and typically, um, those are, uh, among other things is having to connect to comm to our communities and our Maori communities. Uh, happy to take any questions if there are any Ua Thank you very much, uh, for your submission.

And we do have some time for questions if, if members have any. I just had a question around, um, the Iwi Maori, uh, points that you had raised in your submission. This is something that has come up a lot in other submissions as well. Uh, you, you'll see in the act there is, um, provision for, uh, representatives to be appointed onto the management group.

Do you think that that is, um, sufficient from your purposes? And if not, what would you see? And I know you've got, um, really amazing written submission where you've gone through some of this, but what would be the key, um, barriers from, uh, that, or would you, would you like to see that? In what key way would you like to see that extended? Katie, do you have any view on that?

My name is Karen Ward. I'm an independent researcher and practitioner with a background in disaster risk and resilience and lived experience across disability, health, education, community support, local government, and emergency affected environments.

I'm here because the gaps this bill is trying to address and not abstract to me, there are patterns I've seen and lived alongside for many years. First, we need clear statutory duties for more than 25 years. Inquiry after inquiry has shown that when responsibilities are vague, practice becomes inconsistent, and people fall through the cracks.

National consistency isn't optional, it's foundational. Second, continuity of care and equity must be explicitly recognized. We know who is disproportionately harmed when systems fail. Disabled people, older adults, medically dependent individuals. Children, migrants, and others who rely on stable supports.

These are not each cases, they are core populations whose needs must be built into the system, not left a chance. Third, we need structural cross-sector coordination. Emergency management cannot function in isolation. It relies on health, disability services, education, welfare, lifeline, utilities, and community organizations all being able to work together, not just in principle, but in practice.

And this is where the bill must act as both a statutory anchor and a statutory bridge. The bill needs to anchor the emergency relevant duties that already exist in other acts because those duties are inevitably activated during declared emergencies. Sudden disasters and prolonged cascading events at the same time.

It needs statutory bridges mechanisms that allow emergency management organizations to reach out and connect with your obligations held by other agencies. Without these bridges, emos cannot lawfully or consistently coordinate across sectors even when the duties already exist elsewhere.

We've seen what happens when these elements are missing. The North Island severe weather events inquiry showed medically dependent people left without essential services. The COVID-19 Royal Commission highlighted disproportionate impacts on disabled people and older adults, and the Canterbury Earthquakes, and Pike River Royal Commissions identified deep failures in cross-sector coordination.

These are long standing, well-documented systemic issues. This bill is a rare opportunity to finally address them by giving the system both the anchor and the bridges it needs to function. ENC closing. I support the intent of the bill, but it strengths will depend on whether statutory duties, continuity of care, equity, and cross sector coordination are embedded clearly and consistently.
